coredata.dc:title: Enhanced supercapacitor performance of Bi2O3 by Mn doping
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Yixian
Chu, Chen
Li, Yaxi
Deng, Peiji
Liu, Yunliang
Wu, Ruqiang
Liu, Xiang
Zheng, Yunhe
Zhang, Wei
Wu, Jianchun
Li, Haitao
Kang, Zhenhui
coredata.dc:description: High-performance electrode material Mn-doped Bi2O3 with a nanorod morphology is synthesized by a simple soft chemical method. Such material keeps the high stability and high ion conduction efficiency of Bi2O3, while its specific capacity is also enhanced by Mn ions doping treatment. The doping of Mn ions effectively increases the number of oxygen vacancies, modifying the local electron structure, promoting the charge transfer and ion migration of the electrode, leading to high energy density and power density. The mechanism of the electron structure and transfer property affected by Mn doping are also investigated by experimental and theoretical processes. Based on nickel foam substrate, the specific capacitance of the Mn-doped Bi2O3 electrode can reach 1295.6 F g−1 with a current density of 1 A g−1. It also has a high energy density of 149.25 Wh kg−1 and a high power density of 864 W kg−1. Furthermore, Mn-doped Bi2O3 also shows good cycle stability of metal oxide, which can maintain 100% coulomb efficiency and 98% cycle retention rate after 5000 cycles.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Supercapacitor
Bismuth oxide
Mn doping

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of Bi2O3, MnO2 and Mn-doped Bi2O3

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China
Science and Technology Planning Social Development Project of Zhenjiang City

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Guangxi Key Laboratory of Electrochemical Energy Materials, Science and Technology Planning Social Development Project of Zhenjiang City

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grants 51802126 , 52072152 ), the Jiangsu University Jinshan Professor Fund and the Jiangsu Specially-Appointed Professor Fund, Open Fund from Guangxi Key Laboratory of Electrochemical Energy Materials, Science and Technology Planning Social Development Project of Zhenjiang City , Grant SH2019010 . We also appreciate the suggestions from Dr. Yingxue Cui regarding to the electrochemical results analysis.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) The flow chart of synthetizing Bi2O3, MnO2 and Mn-doped Bi2O3, and the SEM images of (b) Bi2O3, (c) MnO2 and (d) Mn-doped Bi2O3.

(a) XRD pattern of the samples of Mn-doped Bi2O3 and two reference materials (Bi2O3 and MnO2), and images of one sample taken by (b) conventional TEM and (c) high-resolution TEM. The element mapping is also made on a different sample whose backscattered TEM image is shown in (d) and the distribution of (e) Mn, (f) O and (g) Bi are displayed.
XPS of (a) survey, (b) Bi 4f, (c) Mn 2p and O 1s of (d) Bi2O3, (e) MnO2 and (f) Mn-doped Bi2O3.
Position of Mn on Bi2O3 surface and lattice and the corresponding charge distribution (the isosurface are 0.06e•Å−3). The Mn is located (a) on the surface and (b) inside the lattice.

The total DOS of (a) pure Bi2O3 before absorbing OH-, (b) pure Bi2O3 after absorbing OH-, (c) Mn-Bi2O3 with Mn atom on the surface before absorbing OH-, (d) Mn-Bi2O3 with Mn atom on the surface after absorbing OH-, (g) Mn-Bi2O3 with Mn atom in the lattice before absorbing OH- and (h) Mn-Bi2O3 with Mn atom in the lattice after absorbing OH-. Additionally, (e), (f), (i), (j) are the partial DOS of Mn in system (c), (d), (g) and (h), respectively.

The change of stimulated current intensity during the TPV test of (a) pure Bi2O3 and (b) Mn-doped Bi2O3. From fitting functions, the average electron relaxation time of pure Bi2O3 and Mn-doped Bi2O3 are 1.67 ms and 5.91 ms, respectively. (c) demonstrates the motion of electrons in stimulating process when transient Laser blows up electrons and (d) shows the recovering process that stimulated electrons return in pure Bi2O3. (e)–(g) display the motion of electrons in Mn-doped Bi2O3, while (e) and (f) demonstrate the stimulating process and (g) shows the recovering process.

Comparison of the (a) galvanostatic charge-discharge (GCD) curves (current density of 1 A g−1) and (b) cyclic voltammogram (CV) curves (scan rate of 1 mV s−1) between Mn-doped Bi2O3, Bi2O3 and MnO2, and (c) GCD curves and (d) CV curves of Mn-doped Bi2O3 electrodes at various current densities and scan rates. (e) Capacitance retention and Coulombic efficiency of the device over 5000 charge/discharge cycles at a current density of 10 A g−1. (f)Nyquist plots of Mn-doped Bi2O3 and Bi2O3 electrodes recorded at open-circuit potentials.

(a) CV curves and (b) GCD curves of the HSC at various scan rates and current densities, (c) schematic diagram of the assembled Mn-Bi2O3/NF, (d) demonstration of a LED bulb lighting by four Mn-doped Bi2O3/NF//AC/NF HSC devices connected in series and (e) the mechanism of interaction between doped Mn and OH- (the process occurred in dashed circle in (c)).
the charge of different Mn ions.

head.ce:title: Enhanced supercapacitor performance of Bi2O3 by Mn doping

ce:author.ce:contributor-role: Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing
Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: High-performance electrode material Mn-doped Bi2O3 with a nanorod morphology is synthesized by a simple soft chemical method. Such material keeps the high stability and high ion conduction efficiency of Bi2O3, while its specific capacity is also enhanced by Mn ions doping treatment. The doping of Mn ions effectively increases the number of oxygen vacancies, modifying the local electron structure, promoting the charge transfer and ion migration of the electrode, leading to high energy density and power density. The mechanism of the electron structure and transfer property affected by Mn doping are also investigated by experimental and theoretical processes. Based on nickel foam substrate, the specific capacitance of the Mn-doped Bi2O3 electrode can reach 1295.6 F g−1 with a current density of 1 A g−1. It also has a high energy density of 149.25 Wh kg−1 and a high power density of 864 W kg−1. Furthermore, Mn-doped Bi2O3 also shows good cycle stability of metal oxide, which can maintain 100% coulomb efficiency and 98% cycle retention rate after 5000 cycles.

ce:section.ce:para: With the increasing utilization of fossil fuels, the rising environmental issues drive people turning their attention to renewable clean energy. Accompanying the exploitation of green energy, the methods of utilization, conversion and storage of energy are also developed prosperously [1–7]. As one class of new energy storage techniques in the 21st century, supercapacitors play an important role [3,8] due to their small size [9], large energy capacity [10,11], low cost [12] and long-life characteristics [13,14]. Usually, supercapacitors can be classified into two types: electric double layer capacitor (EDLC) [15,16] and pseudo-capacitor [17]. Among them, the EDLC does not adopt redox reactions during charging and discharging. Instead, the storage of electrons is realized barely by physical adsorption or desorption. Therefore, the quantity of charges that EDLC stores is very limited [18], but it has relatively stable performance and a long service life [19]. In contrast, pseudo-capacitor adopts chemical redox reactions as the strategy of storing and releasing charge, greatly increasing the quantity of transferred charges and thus having higher specific capacities and energy densities [20,21]. However, their service life and cycle stability are still insufficient to meet the requirements of realistic applications [22,23]. Nevertheless, in recent years, hybrid supercapacitors (HSCs) with both high efficiency and long service life have arisen, which use various solutions (commonly strong electrolytes, such as KOH, Na2SO4, etc.) as the electrolyte, carbon as the negative electrode and metal oxide as the positive electrode, respectively. Combining the advantages of EDLC and pseudo-capacitor, HSC becomes a new attractive topic in the energy storage field [24,25].

Metal oxides and their hybrids such as oxides of Mn, Bi, Co, V and Ni [26–28], are well-known electroactive materials used in supercapacitors, Li-ion batteries and the base materials of electrocatalysis, due to their high metal activity and ion conductivity. Among metal oxides, Bi2O3 is often considered as a potential high-performance material for supercapacitors because of its high energy density and excellent redox behavior [29–34]. Bi2O3 is one of the major forms of Bi in nature and it has multiple crystal structures, depending on the synthesis process and temperatures. The various structures lead to various properties and functions, therefore, Bi2O3 has a wide range of applications, such as optical materials, electronic materials, industrial catalysts, etc [6,35,36]. However, a problem for pure Bi2O3 used in supercapacitor is that its crystal structure degrades rapidly during the electrochemical cycle. Therefore, there are few high-performance records in previous studies of pure Bi2O3 [37]. To enhance the stability of Bi2O3, many investigations have been carried out, among which Mn-based materials (especially MnO2) are frequently adopted to improve the electrochemical properties of pure Bi2O3 via compositing, since Mn has many attractive advantages such as good conductivity and excellent charge storage ability as transition metal. In 2015, Ma et al. reported that the Bi2O3@MnO2 composite (nanosheet MnO2 sticks to the micro-scale Bi2O3 sphere) was synthesized and applied to the supercapacitor, exhibiting excellent electrochemical properties [38]. However, in the past studies, few works used Mn as a doping element to study Bi-based capacitors.

In the present work, a synthesis method of Mn-doped Bi2O3 is introduced. The Mn-doped Bi2O3 synthesized by this method has unique dendrite morphologies and high performance as the capacitor electrode, and for revealing the mechanism of their high performance, the first-principles density functional theory (DFT) calculations are applied. DFT is proven a powerful tool in analyzing energy and electron interaction in microstructures, casting light to the reasons behind phenomena [39–45]. It is shown in this work that, the doping of Mn ions in Bi2O3 not only modifies microstructures of Bi2O3, but also brings good stability and better capacitance to the final assembled capacitor, and the transition between different oxidation valence of Mn ions in the Bi2O3 matrix is the key reason of capacitance improvement of Mn-Bi2O3 electrode.

Bismuth (III) nitrate pentahydrate, manganese (II) nitrate tetrahydrate, potassium hydroxide, sodium hydroxide, Nafion 117 (5 wt%), Pluronic P-123 (EO20PO70EO20), tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS) and n-butanol were purchased from Sinopharm Group. All chemicals used in this study were analytical grade and were used without further purification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of Bi2O3, MnO2 and Mn-doped Bi2O3
ce:section.ce:para: Bi2O3, MnO2 and Mn-Bi2O3 are synthesized by essentially identical methods, as shown in Fig. 1a. In this method, the target material grows on a silica template, therefore, the method to produce silica template is introduced first.

The silica template with a large specific surface area was synthesized by hydrothermal method and hydrolysis method. Firstly, P-123 (6 g) was dissolved in dilute hydrochloric acid (217 g of water and 11.8 g of concentrated hydrochloric acid), and the solution was stirred under constant temperature (35 °C). In the mixed solution, n-butanol (6 g) and ethyl orthosilicate (12.9 g) were added dropwise. Subsequently, the obtained solution was sealed and stirred slowly under 35 °C for 24 h. The formed gelatinous solution was transferred to an autoclave and hydrothermally treated at 100 °C for 24 h. Afterward, the obtained solution was filtered and dried. The dried sample was then immersed in ethanol-hydrochloric acid solution (20 ml ethanol and 2 ml concentrated hydrochloric acid) for 20 min. Finally, the processed sample was washed with deionized water and dried to obtain a white mesoporous silica template in form of powder [46].

After obtaining silica template, the next step is the synthesis of Bi2O3, MnO2 and Mn-Bi2O3. For Bi2O3, first, 0.75 g of bismuth nitrate and 0.2 g of silica template were dissolved in 10 ml of absolute ethanol. After stirring at 25 °C for 3 h, the solution was then evaporated in an oven. After the ethanol is removed by evaporation, the metal ions were impregnated into the channels of the silica template. Then, the sample was put into a tube furnace to anneal at 400 °C for 2 h for oxidizing and fixing the metal ions. Afterward, the obtained powder was washed 3 times with 2 M NaOH and deionized water. Finally, the samples were collected by centrifugation.

The synthesis process of MnO2 is nothing different from that of Bi2O3, except replacing 0.75 g of bismuth nitrate by 0.125 g of manganese nitrate. If 0.75 g of bismuth nitrate and 0.125 g of manganese nitrate are used simultaneously, then the product will be Mn-doped Bi2O3.

Field emission scanning electron microscope (JSM-7800F) and transmission electron microscopic (JEOL 2200FS) were used to observe the morphology of the samples. X-ray diffractometer (XRD-6100) was used to identify the crystal structures of the products on a Bruker D8 diffractometer in the 2θ range of 10–80° at room temperature (Cu Kα radiation, λ = 1.5418 Å). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed on Thermo ESCALAB 250 to analyze the distribution of elements and their valence in samples.
The HSC was designed as such that Mn-doped Bi2O3 is the positive electrode, and the activated carbon (AC) is the negative electrode, and the 2 M KOH solution is used as the electrolyte.

The method to prepare positive working electrode is as follow. The obtained Mn-doped Bi2O3 and a small amount of carbon black were dropped on the nickel foam (NF) electrode to combine the skeleton structure of the NF. First of all, 4 mg of sample (Mn-doped Bi2O3 synthesized in this work) and 1 mg of carbon black were dispersed in a solution (480 μl of isopropanol and 480 μl of water), and then 40 μl of Nafion solution was added in. The mixed solution was sonicated for 3 h to obtain a uniform black ink-like substance, which was then applied dropwise to a 1 × 1 cm2 NF sheet. The painted NF sheet was dried and used as the positive working electrode [47].

The negative electrode is a conventional AC/NF electrode. The ink containing 70 wt% AC, 20 wt% carbon black and 10 wt% dispersant was mixed by sonicating or grinding to make individual components homogeneous, and the mixture was then scribbled to the NF sheet and dried.

The prepared Mn-Bi2O3/NF and AC/NF sheets were then cut into 13 mm-diameter-disks as electrodes, between which 200 μl of 2 M KOH was used as the electrolyte. These electrodes and electrolyte were eventually assembled as a button battery to light up the LED bulb (Fig. 8d), and Whatman Grade GF/D glass microfiber filter is selected as the separator between two electrodes [48].

The electrochemical tests were carried out via Shanghai Chenhua Electrochemical Workstation performed under a three-electrode system, employing Ag/AgCl (3 M KCl) as the reference electrode, platinum electrode as the counter electrode, and 2 M KOH as the electrolyte, respectively. The electrochemical impedance was tested in the frequency range of 100–0.01 kHz at open circuit potential. The specific capacitance C (F g−1), energy density E (Wh kg−1) and the power density P (W kg−1) are calculated by the following formula [49]:
(1)
C =
I ∆ t
m ∆ V
(2)
E =
1
/
2
C
Δ V
2
(3)
P =
E
/
∆ t
Where I (A) is the current, Δt (s) is the discharge time, m (g) is the mass of the adsorbed sample, and ΔV (V) is the discharge voltage range.

The following mass balancing equation was used to determine the charge balance between positive and negative electrodes:
(4)
m
+
m
−
=
C
−
∆ V
−
C
+
∆ V
+
where the subscript + and – indicate positive and negative electrodes, respectively. In this equation, m+ is the mass of Mn-Bi2O3, and m- is the mass of AC, while C is the specific discharge capacitance and ΔV is the discharge voltage range.

The XPS data can be used to analyze the atomic fraction of Mn doped along the surface of Bi2O3. The concentration of each element is calculated by the following equations [50]:
(5)
C
Bi
=
A
Bi
/
S
Bi
A
Bi
/
S
Bi
+
A
Mn
/
S
Mn
+
A
O
/
S
O
(6)
C
Mn
=
A
Mn
/
S
Mn
A
Bi
/
S
Bi
+
A
Mn
/
S
Mn
+
A

/
S
O
(7)
C
O
=
A
O
/
S
O
A
Bi
/
S
Bi

A
Mn
/
S
Mn
+
A
O
/
S
O
where CBi, CMn and CO are the concentration of Bi, Mn and O, and ABi, AMn and AO are the integral area of Bi, Mn and O peak of XPS, while SBi, SMn and SO are atomic sensitivity factors of Bi, Mn and O, respectively. The sensitivity factors adopted the data given in [51].

The first-principles calculations are carried out by Vienna ab initio Simulation Package (VASP) [52]. The pseudo-potential adopts the projected augmented wave (PAW) method [53,54] and the exchange-correlation functional is Perdew-Burke-Ernzerhof (PBE) type generalized gradient approximation (GGA) [55]. The energy cut-off is set as 400 eV, and the structures are relaxed until the atomic force is less than 0.05 eV/Å. Based on the outputs of DFT calculations, Bader charge analysis [56] is applied to characterize the charge information of ions.

Before applying other tests and analysis, the material synthesized in this work was firstly identified by X-ray diffraction (XRD), and the diffraction spectrum is shown in Fig. 2a. The result shows that the diffraction peaks appear at 2θ = 27.9°, 30.2°, 32.6°, 46.2°, 54.2°, 55.4°, and the observed diffraction peaks can be indexed to the (201), (211), (220), (222), (203), (421) planes of Bi2O3. Due to the low concentration of doped Mn (5.7 at% calculated from XPS results), there is no obvious diffraction peak of MnO2 in the XRD. Therefore, it can be deduced that the major component of the material is Bi2O3, while Mn was contained inside in the form of solution atoms, rather than a secondary phase (MnO2). We noticed that the existence of Mn ions slightly shifts the spectrum of Mn-doped Bi2O3 to higher angle than that of Bi2O3, i.e. the lattice of Bi2O3 shrinks because of Mn ions whose size is smaller than Bi ions. However, the shrinkage of the lattice can be ignored since it is too hard to find the shift of spectrum unless comparing the enlarged XRD data carefully.

To confirm the conclusions obtained from XRD and further investigate the habit plane of materials, TEM is adopted. Fig. 2b and c correspondingly display the TEM image of the synthesized Mn-doped Bi2O3 under low and high resolution. The observed main structures are Bi2O3 with different crystal directions. In Fig. 2c, the distances between two neighboring crystal planes are 0.4027 nm and 0.3614 nm, respectively, indicating that the observed planes are (020) and (120). The two planes are not equivalent, therefore, the crystallography plane of the main surfaces of different samples might be various.

Elemental mapping in Fig. 2e–g displays the distribution of three elements in Mn-Bi2O3. As observed in Fig. 2f and g, the O and Bi ions distribute uniformly over the entire particle. The mapping of Mn element indicates that the distribution of Mn is uniform, too. However, the concentration of Mn is too low that noise can be detected, making Mn’s signal distributed throughout the whole image, but the main concentrated Mn area was in the central particle position, as shown in Fig. 2e. A similar condition occurs in rich-contented O (Fig. 2f), which is attributed to the existence of oxygen on carbon film covering the copper grids substrate. All these characterization results show that the material synthesized in the present work is Bi2O3 with uniformly doped Mn ions.

Since the morphology and surface area are crucial for the performance of electrode materials, SEM images of Mn-doped Bi2O3 and two reference materials (Bi2O3 and MnO2) synthesized in the present work are taken to observe the morphologies. It can be seen from Fig. 1b and c that, the Bi2O3 has a morphology of dendritic structure with smooth and dense surface, while the MnO2 appears as loosely packed small particles. However, the Mn-doped Bi2O3 remains dendrite morphology but with a rough surface, as shown in Fig. 1d. The rough surface indicates a large surface area and thus benefits electrochemical performance.

It is notable that silica template has a significant impact on the morphology of Mn-doped Bi2O3. The Mn-doped Bi2O3 synthesized without silica template are micron rods whose diameters are close to those of Mn-doped Bi2O3 dendrites, but with a much smooth surface (Fig. S1). It is expected that the surface area of smooth rods is much smaller than that of rough dendrites, which is reflected by their differences in electrochemical performance. The GCD and CV curves proved that, compared with capacitor adopts Mn-doped Bi2O3 which is synthesized without silica template, the capacitor using silica-template-aid synthesized Mn-doped Bi2O3 shows greatly better electrochemical performance (Fig. S2).

The valence of different elements in Mn-doped Bi2O3 is also concerned since it plays a vital role in the redox reaction. Therefore, XPS analysis is applied and the results are shown in Fig. 3 . The existence of Mn, O, and Bi elements in the composite can be easily confirmed in Fig. 3a and the two shrinking peaks in Fig. 3b at 164.1ev and 158.8ev correspond to the spin orbits of Bi 4 f5/2 and Bi 4 f7/2 respectively, which indicate the presence of Bi3+ ions [57,58]. The Mn 2p spectrum in Fig. 3c has two shrinking peaks at 653.5 eV and 641.9 eV, corresponding to the spin orbital peaks of Mn 2p1/2 and Mn 2p3/2, which respectively indicates the existence of Mn3+ ions [59] . However, although we come to this conclusion from the fitted curve, the spectrum is too noisy to essentially rule out existence of Mn ions with other valence. According to Fig. 3d–f, there are three peaks of O 1 s that can be detected in total in Bi2O3, MnO2 and Mn-doped Bi2O3, namely, O1, O2 and O3, corresponding to lattice oxygen, oxygen vacancy and chemisorbed oxygen species respectively [60]. Since the material is mainly composed of Bi2O3, the unique O3 peak in MnO2 does not appear in Mn-doped Bi2O3. Besides, the O2 peak of Mn-doped Bi2O3 is much bigger than those of Bi2O3 and MnO2 by calculating the area of the fitted curves, which means that the oxygen vacancies of the product increased obviously after Mn doping. The increase of oxygen vacancies is not unexpected, since the stable state of Mn is MnO2, but in Bi2O3 lattice, it is forced to be MnO1.5, which is equivalent to creating a 0.5 oxygen loss in structure when Mn3+ converts to Mn4+ under perturbation. Besides the effect of Mn, the defects of lattice are also important sources of oxygen vacancies as well. From the XPS result, it can be calculated via Eqs. (5)–(7) that the concentration of Bi, Mn and O on the surface of the material are 29.9 at%, 5.7 at% and 64.4 at%, respectively.

The different stable oxygen valence between Bi and Mn ions does not only create more oxygen vacancies, but also affects the absorption of OH- on Mn-doped Bi2O3 in a more profound way. DFT calculations show that doped Mn ions do not always benefit the absorption of OH- on Bi2O3 surface, instead, it is greatly dependent on whether Mn ions are exposing on the Bi2O3 surface or buried in Bi2O3 lattice. Two different positions of doped Mn in Bi2O3 are shown in Fig. 4a and b, where the Mn atom locates on the surface and at the lattice of Bi2O3, respectively. The surface adopted in calculations is (01
1
̅
) plane, as shown in the bottom of Fig. 22c, in which the distance between (120) (equivalent to (021)) planes is cited. The OH- absorption energy of three conditions are calculated. Besides the two conditions corresponding to two types of position of Mn, the condition of pure Bi2O3 is also calculated to make a comparison, and the results are shown in Table 1. According to Table 1, when Mn ion is exposed on the Bi2O3 surface, the OH- binding energy is about 0.2eV less than that of pure Bi2O3, i.e. it slightly suppresses the absorption of OH-. In contrast, when Mn ion is buried inside the lattice, it greatly improves the absorption of OH- with an increase of 0.8eV in OH- binding energy. Assumed that Mn atoms distribute uniformly in Bi2O3, the overall effect of the Mn atoms bringing to the material is supposed to be the improvement of OH- absorption, which matches the experimental examination. However, Mn atoms energetically preferred to expose on the surface, and an energy penalty of 1.7eV will arise if it locates inside the Bi2O3. As a consequence, it is expected that, if Mn-Bi2O3 is heated under elevated temperature for a long time, the Mn ions will gradually migrate to the surface and the ability of OH- absorption of the material will continuously deteriorate.

To study the reasons of property difference caused by Mn-position, the density of state (DOS) and charge of ions are calculated. The total DOS of six systems are shown in Fig. 5, and in the Mn-doped systems, the partial DOS of Mn atom are also plotted. The DOS of Mn-doped Bi2O3 show that, compared to pure Bi2O3 which has no state above Fermi energy until 1.88 eV, Mn introduces an energy state slightly higher than Fermi energy, which indicates that the Mn-doped Bi2O3 have better conductivity. Furthermore, in Mn-doped Bi2O3, the energy states around Fermi level are dominated by d-orbitals of Mn atoms, and some hybridization may occur since the peak of total DOS is higher than the same peak in d-orbitals of Mn. The d-orbitals dominating phenomenon of Mn-Bi2O3 remains when an OH- is absorbed, however, the peaks among Fermi energy of d-orbitals of Mn and thus the whole material, are significantly modified. These DOS show critical information that, although doped Mn is far from the major component, it dominates the absorption of OH- of Mn-Bi2O3 system.

To analyze the impact of OH- on Mn ions, a Bader charge analysis is applied. The electrons of Mn ions in four conditions are listed in Table 2, and to make a comparison, two stable states of Mn, MnO and MnO2, which have + 2 and + 4 valence, respectively, are also analyzed. It can be seen from Table 2 that, Mn2+ or Mn4+ has 5.638 or 5.237 electrons, respectively. Before absorbing an OH-, the Mn on Bi2O3 surface has electrons closed to that of Mn2+, but it has 5.437 electrons when it is buried inside Bi2O3, which is right in the middle of Mn2+ and Mn4+. It is reasonable since the Mn ion in Bi2O3 is forced to accommodate with the same number of coordinated oxygen atoms as Bi, and thus the valence of Mn is expected to be + 3. Consequently, the number of electrons belongs to such Mn should be between those in Mn2+ and Mn4+. A valence of + 3 is an energetically unfavored state for Mn, and thus Bi2O3 with a buried Mn has higher energy than that has Mn on its surface. However, when an OH- is absorbed, it will first release its charge to circuit under the external bias and become OH* , and as compensation, the oxygen of OH* attracts the local electrons of Mn-doped Bi2O3. The electrons of Mn ions in both conditions (surface and lattice) are abstracted, and the buried Mn3+ approach to stable Mn4+ because of loss of electrons, stabilizing the OH* . In contrast, the Mn2+ on the surface becomes high energy Mn3+, which suppresses the absorption of OH-. Therefore, the different positions of the Mn atom in Bi2O3 lead to its different impacts on the absorption of OH-.

The effects of Mn ions on the electrochemical properties of Bi2O3-based electrode material were also raised by the in-situ TPV (transient photo-induced voltage) test, which is a powerful tool to understand the interface-charge transfer kinetics [61,62]. The TPV test result is shown in Fig. 6a and b. According to Fig. 6a, under the same condition, the Mn-doped Bi2O3 exhibits a lower TPV signal intensity than that of pure Bi2O3. The average electron relaxation time obtained from fitting second-order exponential function shows that Mn-doped Bi2O3 requires a longer time (5.91 ms) than pure Bi2O3 (1.67 ms) to achieve equilibrium, indicating that Mn ions retard both the escape process and return process of electrons. A more detailed analysis of the TPV test is given in Supplementary Information. The phenomenon can be further explained by the conclusions obtained from DFT calculations that Mn ions are in an unstable electronic state. As shown in Fig. 6c and d, both in the stimulating processes and the recovering process in pure Bi2O3, the major resistance of electron motions is spatial effect from Bi and O atoms. In contrast, in Mn-doped Bi2O3, Mn3+ captures some electrons and becomes stable Mn2+(Fig. 6e and f), resulting in a reduction of TPV signal intensity. And when the electron structure of the sample recovers, as shown in Fig. 6g, Mn2+ retards this process because it will lose an electron and return back to high-energy Mn3+. The slower discharge rate of Mn-doped Bi2O3 may benefit the material as electrodes for supercapacitors.

Using KOH as the electrolyte, a standard three-electrode system was set to test electrochemical properties of Mn-Bi2O3, Bi2O3 and MnO2. In Fig. 7a, at a current density of 1 A•g−1, the charge and discharge time of Mn-doped Bi2O3 are much longer than that of Bi2O3 and MnO2, which reaches 1496 s. As shown in Fig. S3 and Fig. S4, When the content ratio of bismuth to manganese is 6:1, the oxygen vacancy area will be the largest and the specific capacitance will be the highest. The comparation of Ragone plots is shown in Fig. S5 and its energy density almost reached the performance requirements of commercial capacitors [63]. It proves that under the doping of Mn, the electrochemical performance of Bi2O3 is greatly enhanced. Fig. 7b is the CV curves of Mn-Bi2O3, Bi2O3, and MnO2 at a scan rate of 1 mV•s−1. The integral area of Mn-Bi2O3 is larger than that of Bi2O3 and MnO2, indicating better capacitance. The redox process corresponding to its redox peak is as follows [33]:
(5)
Mn
3 +
→
Mn
4 +
+
e
−
(6)
Mn
2 +
+
e
−
→
Mn
4 +
(7)
B
i
2
O
3
+ 2 O
H
−
→ 2 Bi
O
2
−
+
H
2
O
(8)
Bi
O
2
−
+
e
−
→ Bi
O
2
2 −
(9)
3 Bi
O
2
2 −
+ 2
H
2
O → 2 Bi
O
2
−
+ 4 O
H
−
+ Bi
(10)
2 Bi + 6 O
H
−
→ B
i
2
O
3
+
3 H
2
O +
6 e
−

Fig. 7c is the GCD curve of Mn-doped Bi2O3 at the current density of 1 A g−1, 2 A g−1, 5 A g−1 and 10 A g−1 (the max potential is 0.48 V). Correspondingly, the discharge specific capacitances are 1295.6 F g−1, 1113.2 F g−1, 1034.4 F g−1 and 952 F g−1, demonstrating a good rate performance, and from Eq. (2) it can be calculated that the energy density under 1 A g−1 is 149.25 Wh kg−1. The CV curve of Mn-doped Bi2O3 is performed at the scan rate of 1–100 mV s−1 (Fig. 7d). All of these CV curves have similar shapes, which indicate the high electrochemical reversibility and good rate capacity [37,64]. In the experiment, Mn-doped Bi2O3 was circulated 5000 times at a current density of 10 A g−1, as shown in Fig. 7e, the capacitance retention was about 98% and the coulombic efficiency was still about 100%. We also use electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) to examine the impedance of the materials. The test frequency is from 10 mHz to 100 kHz, and the open circuit voltage is used as the test voltage, and the results are shown in Fig. 7f. According to Fig. 7f, there is no obvious semicircle in the high frequency region of the three curves, and the straight line in the low frequency region indicates the typical capacitance behavior.

For further test of performance, an HSC device was assembled by Mn-Bi2O3/NF (+) and AC/NF (-) electrode, as displayed in Fig. 8c. Before assembling, the electrochemical performance of AC electrode was tested. The roughly rectangular shape of CV curve shows its EDLC characteristics, while the roughly triangular shape of GCD curve indicates its good coulomb efficiency (Fig. S6). Besides, the specific capacitance of the AC electrode can reach 673 F g−1 at a current density of 1 A g−1. The CV curves and GCD curves of assembled HSC are shown in Fig. 8. The CV curves under different scan rates have similar shapes, indicating the high electrochemical reversibility and good rate capacity of the HSC. At the current density of 1 A g−1, the specific capacitance based on the total mass of two electrodes, which were calculated according to Eq. (1), can reach 158 F g−1, while the energy density and power density can also achieve 49.4 Wh kg−1 and 750 W kg−1. respectively. All the data above demonstrate that Mn-doped Bi2O3 is a potential material for HSC and it deserves further investigation. The mechanism of Mn-aid adsorption of OH- is shown in Fig. 8e.

In this work, we successfully synthesized a Mn-doped Bi2O3 composite material and fabricated a high-performance working electrode based on NF. The Mn-Bi2O3 electrode has excellent charge storage capacity (1295.6 F g−1), high energy density (149.25 Wh kg−1) and superior cycle stability, which indicates that Mn ions can improve the electrochemical properties of Bi2O3 effectively. It inherits the high stability and ion conduction efficiency of Bi2O3, and simultaneously possesses the better specific capacitance and conductivity caused by Mn ions, these advantages making it a promising material for high-performance electronic storage device in the future.

Yixian Liu: Writing – original draft, experiment design, calculation, analysis, Writing – review & editing. Chen Chu: Writing – original draft, experiment design, Investigation, data collecting. Yaxi Li: Investigation, data collecting, Visualization. Peiji Deng: Writing – review & editing. Yunliang Liu: Investigation. Ruqiang Wu: Investigation. Xiang Liu: Resources, Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Yunhe Zheng: Analysis. Wei Zhang: Analysis. Jianchun Wu: Calculation, Writing – review & editing. Haitao Li: Group leader, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition. Zhenhui Kang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grants 51802126, 52072152), the Jiangsu University Jinshan Professor Fund and the Jiangsu Specially-Appointed Professor Fund, Open Fund from Guangxi Key Laboratory of Electrochemical Energy Materials, Science and Technology Planning Social Development Project of Zhenjiang City, Grant SH2019010. We also appreciate the suggestions from Dr. Yingxue Cui regarding to the electrochemical results analysis.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: From ultrasoft pseudopotentials to the projector augmented-wave method
